content:
# Numbers Chapter 9 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 158–159; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 119 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_009_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_009_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_009_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — FROM LEVITICAL CONSECRATION TO THE SECOND PASSOVER AND THE CLOUD:** Numbers 9 divides into two distinct sections of vastly different character. The first (vv. 1–14) addresses a specific liturgical problem — what happens when individuals are ritually unclean or on a distant journey and cannot observe the Passover at its appointed time? YHWH provides a legal remedy: a second Passover on the fourteenth day of the second month. This section is notable as one of the few Torah passages where legislation arises directly from a case brought by the people rather than from divine initiative. The second section (vv. 15–23) shifts to a sweeping narrative description of the cloud that covered the Tabernacle — the visible manifestation of YHWH's presence that governed all of Israel's encampments and journeyings in the wilderness. In the Leningrad Codex, this chapter begins on page 158 (the same page where chapter 8 concludes with the Levites' age-of-service provisions) and continues onto page 159. The Vaticanus LXX (p. 119) follows the MT closely, with the chapter visible in the first and second columns of pa_0119. The parashah marker פ (open parashah) appears between v. 14 and v. 15 in the Leningrad Codex, confirming the two-part structure.

---

## The Passover in the Wilderness of Sinai (vv. 1–5)

**1** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh] in-the-wilderness of [be-midbar] Sinai [Sinai], in-the-year [ba-shanah] the-second [ha-shenit] of-their-going-out [le-tzetam] from-the-land of [me-eretz] Egypt [Mitsrayim], in-the-month [ba-khodesh] the-first [ha-rishon], saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** בַּשָּׁנָה הַשֵּׁנִית (ba-shanah ha-shenit) = "in the second year" — this is chronologically prior to the census of Numbers 1:1 (which is dated to the second month of the second year). The Passover command here is dated to the first month, making this passage chronologically earlier than the opening of the book. The Talmud (Pesachim 6b) derives from this that אֵין מוּקְדָם וּמְאוּחָר בַּתּוֹרָה ("there is no [strict] earlier or later in the Torah") — the Torah is not always arranged in chronological order. **LXX:** καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ Σινα ἐν τῷ ἔτει τῷ δευτέρῳ ἐξελθόντων αὐτῶν ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ἐν τῷ μηνὶ τῷ πρώτῳ λέγων ("and [the] Lord spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai in the second year of their going out from [the] land of Egypt, in the first month, saying").

**2** "And-shall-make [ve-ya'asu] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] [et] the-Passover [ha-Pesakh] at-its-appointed-time [be-mo'ado].

> **Key terms:** בְּמוֹעֲדוֹ (be-mo'ado) = "at its appointed time" — from מוֹעֵד (mo'ed, "appointed time/meeting"), the same root as the Tent of Meeting (אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד). The Passover has a fixed calendrical slot — the fourteenth of Nisan at twilight — and this specificity is precisely what creates the legal problem addressed in vv. 6–14. **LXX:** ποιείτωσαν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ τὸ πασχα κατὰ καιρὸν αὐτοῦ ("let the Sons of Israel make the Passover according to its season").

**3** On-the-fourteenth [be-arba'ah asar] day [yom] in-the-month [ba-khodesh] the-this [ha-zeh], between [bein] the-evenings [ha-arbayim], you-shall-make [ta'asu] [et]-it [oto] at-its-appointed-time [be-mo'ado]; according-to-all [ke-khol] its-statutes [khuqqotav] and-according-to-all [u-khe-khol] its-judgments [mishpatav] you-shall-make [ta'asu] [et]-it [oto]."

> **Key terms:** בֵּין הָעַרְבָּיִם (bein ha-arbayim) = "between the evenings" — a technical time-designation debated since antiquity. The Pharisaic/rabbinic tradition (and the Talmud, Pesachim 61a) understands this as the afternoon period from the declining of the sun (after noon) until sunset. The Sadducees and Samaritans understood it as the period between sunset and full darkness. חֻקֹּתָיו ... מִשְׁפָּטָיו (khuqqotav ... mishpatav) = "its statutes ... its judgments" — the doubling ensures that both the ritual prescriptions (חֻקּוֹת, khuqqot, the fixed ceremonial rules) and the case-law rulings (מִשְׁפָּטִים, mishpatim, the judicial determinations) governing Passover must be observed in full. **LXX:** τῇ τεσσαρεσκαιδεκάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ τοῦ μηνὸς τοῦ πρώτου πρὸς ἑσπέραν ποιήσεις αὐτὸ κατὰ καιρούς, κατὰ τὸν νόμον αὐτοῦ καὶ κατὰ τὴν σύγκρισιν αὐτοῦ ποιήσεις αὐτό ("on the fourteenth day of the first month, toward evening, you shall make it according to its seasons, according to its law and according to its ordinance you shall make it").

**4** And-spoke [va-yedabber] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] to-make [la'asot] the-Passover [ha-Pesakh].

**5** And-they-made [va-ya'asu] [et] the-Passover [ha-Pesakh] in-the-first [ba-rishon], on-the-fourteenth [be-arba'ah asar] day [yom] of-the-month [la-khodesh], between [bein] the-evenings [ha-arbayim], in-the-wilderness of [be-midbar] Sinai [Sinai]; according-to-all [ke-khol] which [asher] YHWH commanded [tsivvah] [et] Moses [Mosheh], so [ken] did [asu] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** The obedience formula — כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה כֵּן עָשׂוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל ("according to all which YHWH commanded Moses, so did the Sons of Israel") — confirms that this Passover was performed in full compliance with the Exodus 12 legislation. This is only the second Passover ever observed — the first being in Egypt itself (Exod 12). **LXX:** κατὰ πάντα ὅσα ἐνετείλατο κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ, οὕτως ἐποίησαν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ("according to all things which [the] Lord commanded Moses, so did the Sons of Israel").

---

## The Case of the Unclean Men and the Second Passover (vv. 6–14)

**6** And-there-were [va-yehi] men [anashim] who [asher] were [hayu] unclean [teme'im] by-a-soul of [le-nefesh] a-man [adam], and-not [ve-lo] they-were-able [yakhlu] to-make [la'asot] the-Passover [ha-Pesakh] on-the-day [ba-yom] the-that [ha-hu]; and-they-came-near [va-yiqrevu] before [lifnei] Moses [Mosheh] and-before [ve-lifnei] Aaron [Aharon] on-the-day [ba-yom] the-that [ha-hu].

> **Key terms:** טְמֵאִים לְנֶפֶשׁ אָדָם (teme'im le-nefesh adam) = "unclean by a soul of a man" — i.e., rendered ritually impure through contact with a human corpse. Corpse-impurity (טֻמְאַת מֵת, tum'at met) is the most severe form of ritual contamination, requiring a seven-day purification process (Num 19:11–19). A person in this state is barred from the sanctuary and from participation in sacred meals, including the Passover lamb. The identity of these men is debated: Sifrei (Numbers 69) records two opinions — either Mishael and Elzaphan who carried the bodies of Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:4–5), or men who had carried Joseph's coffin. **LXX:** ἀκάθαρτοι ἐπὶ ψυχῇ ἀνθρώπου ("unclean upon [the] soul of a man").

**7** And-said [va-yomeru] the-men [ha-anashim] the-those [ha-hemmah] unto-him [elav]: "We [anakhnu] [are] unclean [teme'im] by-a-soul of [le-nefesh] a-man [adam]; why [lammah] shall-we-be-diminished [niggara'] so-as-not [le-vilti] to-bring-near [haqriv] [et] the-offering of [qorban] YHWH at-its-appointed-time [be-mo'ado] in-the-midst of [be-tokh] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el]?"

> **Key terms:** לָמָּה נִגָּרַע (lammah niggara') = "why shall we be diminished/held back?" — the niphal of גרע (gara', "to diminish/reduce/withdraw"). These men are not asking for an exemption from Passover; they are asking why their involuntary ritual impurity should cause them to lose the privilege of bringing YHWH's offering. Their question reflects a profound desire to participate in the covenant community's central commemorative act. This is one of the rare Torah passages where legislation emerges from a petition by the people. The other notable cases: the daughters of Zelophehad (Num 27:1–11), the man gathering wood on the Sabbath (Num 15:32–36), and the blasphemer (Lev 24:10–23). **LXX:** ἵνα τί ὑστερήσωμεν προσενέγκαι τὸ δῶρον κυρίῳ κατὰ καιρὸν αὐτοῦ ("in order that why should we fall short of bringing the gift to [the] Lord at its season?").

**8** And-said [va-yomer] unto-them [alehem] Moses [Mosheh]: "Stand [imdu] and-I-shall-hear [ve-eshme'ah] what [mah] YHWH shall-command [yetsavveh] for-you [lakhem]."

> **Key terms:** עִמְדוּ (imdu) = "stand [still]" — the imperative of עמד (amad, "to stand"). Moses does not presume to adjudicate on his own; he instructs the petitioners to wait while he consults YHWH. This models the prophetic-judicial role: when the existing legislation does not clearly cover the case, the matter is brought before YHWH for direct revelation. The same pattern appears in Numbers 15:34 (the Sabbath wood-gatherer) and 27:5 (Zelophehad's daughters). **LXX:** στῆτε καὶ ἀκούσομαι τί ἐντελεῖται κύριος περὶ ὑμῶν ("stand and I shall hear what [the] Lord shall command concerning you").

**9** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**10** "Speak [dabber] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], saying [le'mor]: 'Any-man, any-man [ish ish] who [asher] shall-be [yihyeh] unclean [tame] by-a-soul [la-nefesh] or [o] on-a-journey [be-derekh] far-away [rekhoqah], for-you [lakhem] or-for-your-generations [o-le-doroteikhem], and-he-shall-make [ve-asah] [the] Passover [Pesakh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH].

> **Key terms:** אִישׁ אִישׁ (ish ish) = "any man, any man" — the emphatic doubling functions as a universal distributive: "each and every man." The law extends to two categories: (1) the corpse-impure (טָמֵא לָנֶפֶשׁ, tame la-nefesh) and (2) one on a distant journey (בְּדֶרֶךְ רְחֹקָה, be-derekh rekhoqah). In the Leningrad Codex, the word רְחֹקָה has a dot (puncta extraordinaria) above the final ה — one of the fifteen dotted words in the Torah. The Masorah notes this dot, and the rabbis debated its significance: some understood it to mean that the journey need not actually be "far" but merely far enough to prevent timely arrival at the sanctuary (Pesachim 93b). אוֹ לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (o le-doroteikhem) = "or for your generations" — this law applies not only to the present generation but to all future generations. **LXX:** ἄνθρωπος ἄνθρωπος ὃς ἐὰν γένηται ἀκάθαρτος ἐπὶ ψυχῇ ἀνθρώπου ἢ ἐν ὁδῷ μακρᾷ ὑμῖν ἢ ἐν ταῖς γενεαῖς ὑμῶν, καὶ ποιήσει τὸ πασχα κυρίῳ ("any man who becomes unclean upon [the] soul of a man, or on a far journey, for you or in your generations, he also shall make the Passover to [the] Lord"). The Vaticanus preserves μακρᾷ without any scribal note corresponding to the Hebrew dotted letter.

**11** In-the-month [ba-khodesh] the-second [ha-sheni], on-the-fourteenth [be-arba'ah asar] day [yom], between [bein] the-evenings [ha-arbayim], they-shall-make [ya'asu] [et]-it [oto]; with [al] unleavened-bread [matzot] and-bitter-herbs [u-merorim] they-shall-eat [yokhlu] [et]-it [oto].

> **Key terms:** The second Passover (פֶּסַח שֵׁנִי, Pesakh Sheni) falls exactly one month after the regular Passover: the fourteenth of the second month (Iyyar) instead of the fourteenth of the first month (Nisan). All the core elements of the Passover meal must be observed: unleavened bread (מַצּוֹת, matzot) and bitter herbs (מְרֹרִים, merorim). The sacrificial lamb (implicit in the Passover offering itself) must also be eaten with these accompaniments. **LXX:** ἐπ᾽ ἀζύμων καὶ πικρίδων φάγονται αὐτό ("upon unleavened-bread and bitter-herbs they shall eat it").

**12** They-shall-not [lo] leave [yash'iru] of-it [mimmenu] until [ad] morning [boqer], and-a-bone [ve-etzem] they-shall-not [lo] break [yishberu] in-it [vo]; according-to-all [ke-khol] the-statute of [khuqqat] the-Passover [ha-Pesakh] they-shall-make [ya'asu] [et]-it [oto].

> **Key terms:** The two prohibitions replicate Exodus 12:10 (nothing left until morning) and Exodus 12:46 (no bone broken). The phrase וְעֶצֶם לֹא יִשְׁבְּרוּ בוֹ (ve-etzem lo yishberu vo, "and a bone they shall not break in it") is quoted in John 19:36 with reference to the crucifixion of Jesus, linking the Passover lamb typology to the cross. כְּכָל חֻקַּת הַפֶּסַח (ke-khol khuqqat ha-Pesakh) = "according to all the statute of the Passover" — the second Passover is not an abbreviated version; it carries the full force of the original statute. **LXX:** οὐ καταλείψουσιν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸ πρωὶ καὶ ὀστοῦν οὐ συντρίψουσιν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ· κατὰ τὸν νόμον τοῦ πασχα ποιήσουσιν αὐτό ("they shall not leave of it until the morning and a bone they shall not break of it; according to the law of the Passover they shall make it").

**13** And-the-man [ve-ha-ish] who [asher] he [hu] [is] clean [tahor] and-on-a-journey [u-ve-derekh] not [lo] he-was [hayah], and-he-ceases [ve-khadal] to-make [la'asot] the-Passover [ha-Pesakh] — and-shall-be-cut-off [ve-nikhretah] the-soul [ha-nefesh] the-that [ha-hi] from-her-people [me-ammeiha], for [ki] [the] offering of [qorban] YHWH not [lo] he-brought-near [hiqriv] at-its-appointed-time [be-mo'ado] — his-sin [khet'o] shall-bear [yissa] the-man [ha-ish] the-that [ha-hu].

> **Key terms:** וְנִכְרְתָה (ve-nikhretah) = "and shall be cut off" — the niphal of כרת (karat, "to cut"). The penalty of כָּרֵת (karet, "cutting off") is one of the most severe in the Torah, debated as to whether it means premature death, childlessness, excommunication, or divine punishment in the afterlife. This penalty applies only to one who is ritually clean and not traveling — someone who has no excuse for failing to observe the Passover. The contrast with vv. 10–12 is sharp: those who are unable are given a second chance; those who are able but refuse are cut off. חֶטְאוֹ יִשָּׂא (khet'o yissa) = "his sin he shall bear" — the man bears full culpability. **LXX:** ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχὴ ἐκείνη ἐκ τοῦ λαοῦ αὐτῆς ("that soul shall be utterly destroyed from her people") — a stronger rendering than "cut off," using ἐξολεθρεύω ("to destroy completely").

**14** And-if [ve-khi] shall-sojourn [yagur] with-you [ittkhem] a-stranger [ger] and-he-shall-make [ve-asah] [the] Passover [Pesakh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], according-to-the-statute of [ke-khuqqat] the-Passover [ha-Pesakh] and-according-to-its-judgment [u-khe-mishpato], so [ken] he-shall-make [ya'aseh]; a-statute [khuqqah] one [akhat] shall-be [yihyeh] for-you [lakhem], and-for-the-stranger [ve-la-ger] and-for-the-native of [u-le-ezrakh] the-land [ha-aretz]."

> **Key terms:** גֵּר (ger) = "stranger/sojourner/resident alien" — a non-Israelite who has attached himself to the Israelite community and accepted its covenantal obligations. The ger is distinguished from the נָכְרִי (nokhri, "foreigner") who is explicitly excluded from the Passover (Exod 12:43). The inclusion of the ger under the same statute as the native-born (אֶזְרָח, ezrakh) is a major principle of Israelite law: one Torah for all who dwell within the covenant community. חֻקָּה אַחַת (khuqqah akhat) = "one statute" — a powerful statement of legal equality. This principle recurs at Numbers 15:15–16, 29. **LXX:** νόμος εἷς ἔσται ὑμῖν καὶ τῷ προσηλύτῳ καὶ τῷ αὐτόχθονι τῆς γῆς ("one law shall be for you, and for the proselyte, and for the native of the land") — the LXX renders גֵּר as προσήλυτος ("proselyte," one who has come over to the community) and אֶזְרָח as αὐτόχθων ("one born from the land itself").

---

## The Cloud over the Tabernacle (vv. 15–23)

**15** And-on-the-day of [u-ve-yom] setting-up [haqim] [et] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan], covered [kissah] the-cloud [he-anan] [et] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan], for [le] the-Tent of [Ohel] the-Testimony [ha-Edut]; and-in-the-evening [u-va-erev] there-was [yihyeh] upon [al] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan] like-an-appearance of [ke-mar'eh] fire [esh] until [ad] morning [boqer].

> **Key terms:** הָקִים (haqim) = "setting up" — the hiphil infinitive construct of קום (qum, "to arise/stand"), here meaning "to erect." This refers back to Exodus 40:17 when the Tabernacle was first erected on the first day of the first month of the second year. כִּסָּה הֶעָנָן (kissah he-anan) = "the cloud covered" — the cloud (עָנָן, anan) is the visible manifestation of YHWH's כָּבוֹד (kavod, "glory/weight") as described in Exodus 40:34–38. לְאֹהֶל הָעֵדֻת (le-Ohel ha-Edut) = "for the Tent of the Testimony" — an alternative designation for the Tabernacle, named for the עֵדוּת (edut, "testimony"), i.e., the tablets of the covenant housed within the Ark. כְּמַרְאֵה אֵשׁ (ke-mar'eh esh) = "like an appearance of fire" — at night the cloud's appearance transforms into a fire-like glow. The qualifying כְּ ("like/as") is significant: it is not fire itself but the appearance of fire. **LXX:** καὶ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ᾗ ἐστάθη ἡ σκηνή, ἐκάλυψεν ἡ νεφέλη τὴν σκηνήν, τὸν οἶκον τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("and on the day the tent was set up, the cloud covered the tent, the house of the testimony") — rendering אֹהֶל הָעֵדוּת as "the house of the testimony."

**16** So [ken] it-was [yihyeh] continually [tamid]: the-cloud [he-anan] would-cover [yekhassennu] it, and-an-appearance of [u-mar'eh] fire [esh] [by] night [laylah].

> **Key terms:** תָּמִיד (tamid) = "continually/perpetually" — the same word used for the perpetual lamp (Exod 27:20), the perpetual bread (Exod 25:30), and the perpetual burnt offering (Num 28:6). The cloud's presence is not sporadic but constant — a permanent visible assurance of YHWH's dwelling in the midst of the camp. יְכַסֶּנּוּ (yekhassennu) = "would cover it" — the piel imperfect with third-person suffix, expressing habitual or continuous action. **LXX:** οὕτως ἐγίνετο διὰ παντός· ἡ νεφέλη ἐκάλυπτεν αὐτήν, καὶ εἶδος πυρὸς τὴν νύκτα ("so it was continually: the cloud covered it, and [the] appearance of fire [by] night").

**17** And-according-to [u-le-fi] the-going-up of [he'alot] the-cloud [he-anan] from-upon [me-al] the-Tent [ha-Ohel], and-after-that [ve-akharei khen] would-journey [yis'u] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el]; and-in-the-place [u-vi-mqom] where [asher] would-dwell [yishkon] there [sham] the-cloud [he-anan], there [sham] would-encamp [yakhanu] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** הֵעָלוֹת (he'alot) = "the going up" — the niphal infinitive construct of עלה (alah), describing the cloud's ascent from the Tent. The cloud's movement is entirely sovereign — Israel does not decide when to travel; they follow the cloud. יִשְׁכֹּן (yishkon) = "would dwell" — from שׁכן (shakhan, "to dwell/tabernacle"), the root of שְׁכִינָה (Shekhinah, the rabbinic term for God's indwelling presence) and of מִשְׁכָּן (Mishkan, "Tabernacle/Dwelling"). Where the cloud dwells, Israel dwells. **LXX:** καὶ μετὰ τὸ ἀναβῆναι τὴν νεφέλην ἀπὸ τῆς σκηνῆς καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα ἀπῆραν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ("and after the going up of the cloud from the tent, and after these things the Sons of Israel set out").

**18** Upon [al] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH would-journey [yis'u] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-upon [ve-al] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH they-would-encamp [yakhanu]; all [kol] the-days [yemei] which [asher] the-cloud [he-anan] would-dwell [yishkon] upon [al] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan] they-would-encamp [yakhanu].

> **Key terms:** עַל פִּי יְהוָה (al pi YHWH) = "upon the mouth of YHWH" — literally, "according to the mouth of YHWH," meaning "at YHWH's command." The phrase פִּי (pi, "mouth of") indicates that the cloud's movements are an expression of YHWH's spoken will, not merely a natural phenomenon. The repetition of this phrase (twice in this verse, and recurring in vv. 20, 23) establishes the theological principle: every encampment and every journey is a direct act of divine command. **LXX:** διὰ προστάγματος κυρίου ("through [the] command of [the] Lord") — rendering פִּי as πρόσταγμα ("command/ordinance") rather than στόμα ("mouth").

**19** And-in-the-prolonging [u-ve-ha'arikh] the-cloud [he-anan] upon [al] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan] days [yamim] many [rabbim], and-kept [ve-shameru] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] [et] the-charge of [mishmeret] YHWH and-not [ve-lo] they-journeyed [yissa'u].

> **Key terms:** בְּהַאֲרִיךְ (be-ha'arikh) = "in the prolonging" — the hiphil infinitive construct of ארך (arakh, "to be long"), meaning "when [the cloud] extended/prolonged [its stay]." מִשְׁמֶרֶת (mishmeret) = "charge/guard-duty/obligation" — from שׁמר (shamar, "to keep/guard"). The phrase מִשְׁמֶרֶת יְהוָה (mishmeret YHWH, "the charge of YHWH") means they faithfully kept what YHWH entrusted to them — in this case, the duty of remaining encamped and waiting. Obedience to YHWH includes the discipline of not moving when the cloud does not move. **LXX:** καὶ ὅταν ἐφέλκηται ἡ νεφέλη ἐπὶ τῆς σκηνῆς ἡμέρας πλείους ... καὶ φυλάξουσιν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ τὴν φυλακὴν τοῦ θεοῦ ("and whenever the cloud lingered upon the tent many days ... the Sons of Israel shall keep the watch of God").

**20** And-there-was [ve-yesh] when [asher] the-cloud [he-anan] would-be [yihyeh] days [yamim] of-number [mispar] upon [al] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan] — upon [al] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH they-would-encamp [yakhanu] and-upon [ve-al] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH they-would-journey [yissa'u].

> **Key terms:** יָמִים מִסְפָּר (yamim mispar) = "days of number" — i.e., a [small] counted number of days, a few days. The construct indicates a limited, countable period — contrasting with the "many days" of v. 19. Whether the cloud stayed long or briefly, the response was the same: obedience to YHWH's command. **LXX:** ἡμέρας ἀριθμῷ ("days in number").

**21** And-there-was [ve-yesh] when [asher] the-cloud [he-anan] would-be [yihyeh] from-evening [me-erev] until [ve-ad] morning [boqer], and-the-cloud [he-anan] would-go-up [ve-na'alah] in-the-morning [ba-boqer], and-they-journeyed [ve-nasa'u]; or [o] [for] a-day [yomam] and-a-night [va-laylah], and-the-cloud [he-anan] would-go-up [ve-na'alah], and-they-journeyed [ve-nasa'u].

> **Key terms:** The text considers the shortest possible encampment: a single night (from evening to morning) or a single day-and-night cycle. Even for such brief periods, the camp was fully set up and then dismantled when the cloud lifted. The repetitive structure — cloud stays, they camp; cloud lifts, they journey — reinforces the mechanical, absolute obedience of Israel to the cloud's movements. **LXX:** καὶ ὅταν γένηται ἡ νεφέλη ἀφ᾽ ἑσπέρας ἕως πρωΐ, καὶ ἀναβῇ ἡ νεφέλη τὸ πρωΐ, καὶ ἀπαροῦσιν ἡμέρας ἢ νυκτός ("and whenever the cloud was from evening until morning, and the cloud went up in the morning, they would set out by day or by night").

**22** Or [o] two-days [yomayim] or [o] a-month [khodesh] or [o] days [yamim] — in-the-prolonging [be-ha'arikh] the-cloud [he-anan] upon [al] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan] to-dwell [lishkon] upon-it [alav] — they-would-encamp [yakhanu] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] and-not [ve-lo] they-would-journey [yissa'u]; and-in-its-being-taken-up [u-ve-he'aloto], they-would-journey [yissa'u].

> **Key terms:** יוֹמָיִם אוֹ חֹדֶשׁ אוֹ יָמִים (yomayim o khodesh o yamim) = "two days or a month or [many] days" — the ascending scale (two days ... a month ... an indefinite period) covers every possible duration. לִשְׁכֹּן (lishkon) = "to dwell" — the infinitive construct of שׁכן, emphasizing the cloud's act of dwelling/resting upon the Tabernacle. The cloud's dwelling mirrors YHWH's dwelling: when the cloud rests, God is present; Israel must wait upon his timing. **LXX:** ἡμέρας δύο ἢ μῆνα ἢ ἐνιαυτόν ("two days or a month or a year") — the LXX renders יָמִים ("days") as ἐνιαυτόν ("a year"), interpreting the indefinite "days" as potentially meaning a full year. This is a notable divergence from the MT.

**23** Upon [al] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH they-would-encamp [yakhanu] and-upon [ve-al] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH they-would-journey [yissa'u]; [et] the-charge of [mishmeret] YHWH they-kept [shamaru], upon [al] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH by-the-hand of [be-yad] Moses [Mosheh].

> **Key terms:** The chapter concludes with a threefold repetition of עַל פִּי יְהוָה (al pi YHWH, "upon the mouth of YHWH") — encamping, journeying, and the charge itself all flow from YHWH's direct command. בְּיַד מֹשֶׁה (be-yad Mosheh) = "by the hand of Moses" — YHWH's commands are mediated through Moses. The phrase אֶת מִשְׁמֶרֶת יְהוָה שָׁמָרוּ (et mishmeret YHWH shamaru, "the charge of YHWH they kept") forms an inclusio with v. 19, bookending the cloud-narrative with the theme of faithful obedience. **LXX:** διὰ προστάγματος κυρίου ... τὴν φυλακὴν κυρίου ἐφυλάξαντο διὰ προστάγματος κυρίου ἐν χειρὶ Μωυσῆ ("through [the] command of [the] Lord ... the watch of [the] Lord they kept, through [the] command of [the] Lord, by [the] hand of Moses").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (pp. 158–159)

1. **Chapter boundary:** Numbers 9 begins partway through the right column of page 158 of the Leningrad Codex, following the conclusion of Numbers 8 (the Levites' age-of-service provisions, vv. 23–26). The chapter heading פרשת בהעלתך continues from chapter 8; no new parashah begins at 9:1. The text of Numbers 9 continues through the central and left columns of page 158 and onto page 159.

2. **Open parashah (פ) at v. 15:** A clear parashah petuhah (open section) marker appears between v. 14 and v. 15, confirming the major structural break between the Passover legislation and the cloud narrative. This is one of the most significant section divisions in Numbers 9, as it separates legal material from narrative material.

3. **Dotted letter in v. 10 — רְחֹקָה (rekhoqah):** The word רְחֹקָה ("far away") in v. 10 bears a dot (punctum extraordinarium) above the final ה. This is one of the fifteen extraordinary points (נְקֻדּוֹת, nequddot) in the Torah — ancient scribal marks whose meaning was debated even in the Talmudic period. The standard rabbinic interpretation (Pesachim 93b) is that the dot over the ה indicates that the person need not actually be far away: even one who is just outside the threshold of the Temple courtyard at the time of slaughter is considered "on a distant journey" for the purpose of this law. The dot thus functions as a hermeneutical signal — a scribal flag inviting interpretive inquiry.

4. **Masoretic notation:** The Masorah Parva (Mp) annotations visible in the margins of pages 158–159 include frequency notes on rare forms. The ketiv/qere apparatus is clean for this chapter — no ketiv/qere variants are noted.

### Observations from Codex Vaticanus (p. 119)

5. **LXX divergence in v. 22 — יָמִים rendered as ἐνιαυτόν:** The most significant LXX divergence in this chapter occurs at v. 22, where the Hebrew יָמִים (yamim, "days") is rendered by the Vaticanus as ἐνιαυτόν ("a year"). The Hebrew text lists "two days or a month or days" as the possible durations; the LXX extends the scale to "two days or a month or a year." This may reflect an interpretive tradition that understood the indefinite יָמִים to mean an extended period equivalent to a year (cf. Lev 25:29 where יָמִים means "a full year"), or it may represent an independent textual tradition. The Vaticanus reading is clearly legible in the second column of p. 119.

6. **LXX rendering of כָּרֵת (v. 13):** The Vaticanus renders וְנִכְרְתָה ("and shall be cut off") with ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ("shall be utterly destroyed"), a notably more severe term than a simple "cutting off." This reflects the LXX translators' theological interpretation of the karet penalty as total destruction rather than mere separation from the community.

7. **Consistency in divine-name rendering:** Throughout this chapter, the Vaticanus consistently renders the Tetragrammaton (YHWH) with κύριος (kyrios, "Lord"), following standard LXX practice. The Hebrew פִּי יְהוָה ("mouth of YHWH") becomes διὰ προστάγματος κυρίου ("through the command of the Lord") — replacing the anthropomorphic "mouth" with the abstract "command."

### Theological and Structural Notes

8. **Chronological displacement (v. 1):** Numbers 9:1 is dated to the first month of the second year — earlier than the census of Numbers 1:1, which is dated to the first day of the second month of the second year. This means Numbers 9:1–14 is chronologically prior to Numbers 1. The Talmud's principle אֵין מוּקְדָם וּמְאוּחָר בַּתּוֹרָה ("there is no [strict] earlier or later in the Torah") is derived directly from this passage (Pesachim 6b). The reason for this displacement may be that the Torah wished to begin the book of Numbers with the census (a fitting opening for a book about counting and organization) rather than with a Passover supplement.

9. **The cloud as divine guidance (vv. 15–23):** The repetitive, almost hypnotic prose of vv. 15–23 — with its insistent variations on "when the cloud lifted, they journeyed; when it settled, they encamped" — is a literary device conveying absolute, unvarying obedience. The ninefold repetition of journey-encamp language and the threefold repetition of עַל פִּי יְהוָה ("at the mouth of YHWH") in the final verse create a rhetorical effect of totality: Israel's movements were entirely governed by YHWH's visible presence. This section provides the theological foundation for the journey narratives that follow in Numbers 10–21.

10. **The second Passover as case-law precedent (vv. 6–8):** The narrative structure of vv. 6–8 — petition by the people, Moses' consultation of YHWH, divine response — establishes a model for ad hoc revelation in response to unforeseen circumstances. This same pattern recurs in Numbers 15:32–36 (Sabbath wood-gatherer), Numbers 27:1–11 (daughters of Zelophehad), and Leviticus 24:10–23 (the blasphemer). In each case, the existing law does not clearly address the situation, and new legislation is promulgated through prophetic inquiry. The petitioners of v. 7 are notable for their piety: they do not accept exclusion from the Passover as inevitable but actively seek a remedy — and YHWH honors their desire.
